new_findings.title: Build a fan profile through scripted questions before any PPV

variants.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
variants.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. They ask about: (1) Profession — men spend 40+ hrs/week on work and will talk about it freely; also signals income level. (2) Location — dictates tone, cultural references, and timezone. (3) Age — a 52-year-old has 30 more years of earning history than a 22-year-old; older subs get more formal language, younger subs get slang. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.

variants.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
variants.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.

variants.claim: DM revenue depends on running every fan through a fixed pre-sale framework (data-collection questions, position check, pull-push flirting, then scripted PPV with guilt-trip fallbacks) rather than letting chatters spam PPVs.
variants.what_they_do: Trains new chatters on six new-subscriber questions (name, age, location, work, preferences, kinks) logged to notes, an 'imposition' check that the fan is in a buyable situation, a 'pull-push' flirting loop, then the PPV script, with pre-written guilt-trip messages for each failure point; reports a 75-80% unlock rate.

sources.quote: number one profession you will be talking to men okay simps Subs they are men
another one is age how old are they part of the reason of qualifying is that we want to see their potential as a customer
what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200
that's why my chatter conversions are 75 to 80% plus
you don't want to let the fan see the sales knife
pull fan in with a flirty comment... and then push them away with a generic question

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm for chatter qualification frameworks (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a qualification checklist into the in-house chatter training SOP: chatters must capture profession, location, age, and at least one hobby for every sub before escalating to selling. Store this data per-fan in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9) to enable personalized re-engagement. Age and profession data should also feed the whale-hunting logic — a 50-year-old professional is a higher-priority cultivation target than an 18-year-old student.

new_findings.title: Train chatters on a fixed funnel: questions, imposition, pull-push, guilt trips

variants.claim: DM revenue depends on running every fan through a fixed pre-sale framework (data-collection questions, position check, pull-push flirting, then scripted PPV with guilt-trip fallbacks) rather than letting chatters spam PPVs.
variants.what_they_do: Trains new chatters on six new-subscriber questions (name, age, location, work, preferences, kinks) logged to notes, an 'imposition' check that the fan is in a buyable situation, a 'pull-push' flirting loop, then the PPV script, with pre-written guilt-trip messages for each failure point; reports a 75-80% unlock rate.

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

sources.quote: that's why my chatter conversions are 75 to 80% plus
you don't want to let the fan see the sales knife
pull fan in with a flirty comment... and then push them away with a generic question
We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting with no internal SOP/scripts/funnel (§8 OUTSOURCED); this is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house and AI chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture this questions→imposition→pull-push→script→guilt-trip framework as the backbone SOP for Palm's future in-house chat team and as AI-chat training material.

new_findings.topic: PPV price escalation ladders by fan tier
new_findings.title: Tiered PPV price escalation: 50% steps for low spenders, 70% steps for high spenders

variants.claim: PPV prices should escalate by a set percentage each step within a loop, calibrated to the fan's spending tier, and the $200 OnlyFans cap is not a real ceiling — it can be bypassed via tips or multi-PPV unlocks.
variants.what_they_do: Low spenders (max purchase <$50): increase price ~50% per step. Example ladder: $35 → $55 → $85 → $125. High spenders (max purchase >$120): increase ~70% per step. Example ladder: $50 → $85 → $150 → $255. To exceed the $200 OF cap: (a) request a tip first, then send the PPV, or (b) split into multiple PPVs (e.g. 4×$200 = $800 total) framed as 'unlock these now and I'll send the main video while I get it from my archive.' Always frame multi-unlocks as a favor/bonus. If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: for low spenders, which I would define as someone with a maximum purchase of $50, we can increase the price each time by about 50%
for higher spenders, subscribers with a maximum purchase of over $120, we can try and increase the price by about 70% each time
you can't send a pay-per-view over $200. Sure, you can't, but you can still get around that in other ways
you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or escalation framework (§7 GAP — explicitly called out as the #1 creator concern). This is directly applicable intelligence for building Palm's pricing playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document these two ladders (50% / 70% escalation by tier) as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Add the $200-bypass mechanics (tip-first, multi-unlock bundle) to chatter SOPs. Critically, also adopt the 'add more content rather than discount' rule for objection handling — this protects price integrity over time and should be a standing chatter policy.

new_findings.topic: Onboarding sequence — OF page setup before social access
new_findings.title: Structured 7-day onboarding: OF page + vault first (days 1–4), social access + content plan second (days 5–7)

variants.claim: A sequenced onboarding where the OF page is optimized and chatting infrastructure is ready before social accounts are touched reduces errors and accelerates time-to-revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Days 1–2: get OF login and Inflow access, optimize vault and OF page. Days 3–4: bio, subscription price, page optimization. Days 5–7: gain IG/TikTok/Twitter access, set up content distribution, scripts, chatting setup, and build content plan with creator. Content plan uses the 3-competitor research process (top videos → style selection → post → teach creator what to film and why).

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

sources.quote: Log in to the only fans in the inflow account. Optimize the vault, optimize the OF page. Then on days like seven, I want you to get into the Instagram, Tik Tok, and Twitter accounts
content strategy and create a content plan with her. So on that, I want you to do niche competitor research
you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.
first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding form captures credentials and contract (§6) but there is no documented day-by-day onboarding sequence or ordering logic. The OF vault/PPV system is thin (§2) and no OF page optimization checklist exists. The social setup is mature for AI accounts (§4) but not formalized for real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a real-creator onboarding SOP mirroring the AI warmup cadence structure (§4): Days 1–4 = OF access + page/vault optimization checklist; Days 5–7 = social access + content plan creation. Add this as a checklist workflow in Airtable under the Creators table, triggered on creator status change to 'onboarding.'

new_findings.topic: Client wins database as retention and sales ammo
new_findings.title: Maintain a running 'wins database' — screenshots, metrics, clips — per client for retention and resigning

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

variants.claim: A concrete, numeric performance guarantee (specific result in a specific window or you work for free) reduces perceived risk and differentiates from scam-wary creators' defaults.
variants.what_they_do: Uses guarantees like '2,000 paying subs in 30 days or we work for free' or 'clear buying activity in chats within 14 days or we fund your next shoot up to 1,000 dollars'; pairs the offer with one main angle (speed/risk/ease).

sources.quote: we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'
if you don't get at least 2,000 paying subs in 30 days, we work for free until you do
choose one main angle... speed, risk, and ease

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker tools (§9) for fan-level data, but no documented system for capturing and storing creator wins (viral posts, subscriber milestones, revenue records) as a retention tool. The §9 gap also notes no written retention strategy. A wins database is directly applicable: Palm's editors and social team observe creator wins daily and could log them systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Wins' table in Airtable (Content Ops or HQ base) with fields: creator, win type (milestone sub count, viral reel, PPV revenue record, press mention), media URL, date. Make logging a win a required step for editors/social team when they observe a notable metric. Josh or a creator success role reviews and shares wins back to creators weekly — this doubles as creator retention messaging and provides case study content for acquisition.

new_findings.topic: Outreach message framing — authority + personalization, no direct pitch
new_findings.title: Open with a conversion-problem hook; never pitch the agency in the first DM

variants.claim: The first DM should establish authority by identifying a specific problem in the creator's setup rather than pitching the agency, and should funnel toward a no-pressure discovery call rather than explaining the revenue split.
variants.what_they_do: Template opener: 'Hey [Name], I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions.' Then a brief authority statement. Never mention the split in DMs — save that for the call. On the call: ask current earnings, show what's possible, then close on next steps and send a contract via Dropbox.

variants.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
variants.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

sources.quote: Hey Name, I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions
Don't explain the split on the DMs. Obviously, explain the split on the actual call itself
focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio
I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message template or sales-call funnel (§6 GAP). The split is set per-creator at onboarding but no pre-call qualifying script exists (§7 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Draft a 2–3 message DM sequence template: (1) problem-hook opener with authority, (2) 24-hr follow-up voice note, (3) call-to-action to book a 10–15 min discovery call. Create a call script with qualifying questions (current earnings, goals) before revealing the split. Store both in the acquisition runbook.

new_findings.topic: Quality-first creator sourcing and red-flag filtering
new_findings.title: Identify shared traits of best creators and make acquisition deliberately pickier

variants.claim: Fulfillment problems start at acquisition — a creator who won't send content or holds unrealistic expectations was a selection failure, not a delivery failure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan runs an acquisition audit asking: where do the best creators come from (outbound, inbound, referrals, IG, switching agencies, existing creator networks)? What traits do the best creators share (existing social proof, consistent posting history, strong personality, existing audience, responsiveness, coachability, good on camera)? Which red flags are being ignored at signing? He distinguishes acquisition as a quality game vs. volume game depending on deal structure: pure volume only makes sense if selling a low-ticket course ($497) at scale with upsells; for a full-service agency the quality game dominates. He argues most agencies sign out of desperation to grow the inflow number and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later.

variants.claim: Spending months outreaching for a single high-quality creator yields more revenue and less wasted effort than onboarding an easy-to-sign but unmotivated creator.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly refuses to sign creators who don't film on time, lack motivation, or don't meet appearance standards. He targets creators with 5k–50k followers who already have or look suitable for OnlyFans. He argues a bad 3-month engagement versus a 3-month search for an A-tier creator is a false economy because agency leverage multiplies creator quality.

sources.quote: most agencies are nowhere near picky enough. They're so desperate to sign creators that they just ignore any of the red flags and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later
fulfillment starts at acquisition. You cannot outdeliver a bad creator selection forever
acquisition is a quality game or a volume game depending on the business model, depending on the deal structure
if you were to spend those same exact 3 months outreaching and then sign your A tier creator, you can make not only $10,000, $20,000 a month
the better your creator is, the more your work will show for it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no documented qualification criteria, scoring rubric, or red-flag checklist (§6 GAP). The onboarding form wizard captures name, email, commission %, state, contract — but nothing about creator quality signals. This is one of Palm's biggest identified gaps (§6).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a creator qualification scorecard used before any call is booked: existing monthly OF revenue, social following size and growth rate, content posting consistency (check IG grid), responsiveness in initial DMs, niche fit, willingness to follow direction (ask directly), and whether they've had a previous agency (and why they left). Score each prospect. Set a minimum threshold to proceed to a close call. Add a 'red flags observed' field to the `Creators` Airtable record. This directly addresses §6 GAP and will reduce C-creator drag identified in the retention finding above.

new_findings.title: Source creators from OF-video comment sections and push to a call

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

variants.claim: Find creators by searching niche keywords across multiple platforms, but treat satisfied-client referrals as the highest-quality channel.
variants.what_they_do: Search 'only fans' / niche terms (e.g. gamer girl) on Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok and filter for link-in-bio + recent activity. TikTok surfaces up-and-comers; Reddit is where whales and creators already are. Best channel = referrals: after a great first month in the green, ask the happy creator who else does OnlyFans.

sources.quote: within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away
you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username
first one of course is Instagram... Your other option is going to be Twitter... The next one is going to be Reddit... You can also find them on Tik Tok.
by far the best is referrals... after you've delivered a great service... ask, do you know of anybody else who's also doing Only Fans.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is outbound-DM + referral but with no documented sourcing method, message template, or call-booking step (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a sourcing playbook (comment-section mining of help-seeking creators), a curiosity-led opener, multi-platform outreach, and call-booking as the CTA.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Free teaser → $5–15 qualifier → $30–40+ escalation PPV ladder

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: PPV Looping: Follow up within 5–10 seconds of any purchase to chain sales

variants.claim: The best time to sell a subscriber another PPV is immediately after they make any purchase, capitalizing on the 'f*** it' impulse to keep the buying momentum going.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters monitor their CRM (e.g. Infloww) for any purchase notification — old PPV, mass PPV, feed post unlock — and follow up within 5–10 seconds with a curiosity-hook message before the fan even finishes watching what they bought. The follow-up doesn't reference the old video; it teases the next thing ('I was literally just about to do something I don't usually do'). From that reply, the chatter plans to sell 5 sequential PPVs, each progressively more explicit or longer, so each one appears more valuable and justifies a higher price. This loop restarts every time the fan buys.

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

sources.quote: the best time to sell something to a subscriber is right after they've made a purchase
chatters know the second they get that notification on their inflow or whatever CRM you use, that they need to follow up with the subscriber instantly
I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8). There is no internal SOP, chatter workflow, or CRM trigger process documented. This is a concrete, mechanics-level system that would form the core of Palm's future in-house chat team playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, make PPV looping the foundational selling framework. Define the CRM trigger (purchase notification), set a hard response-time SLA of <30 seconds, write curiosity-hook templates for the follow-up message, and require chatters to plan a 5-PPV ladder before entering any loop. Build this into chatter onboarding SOPs.

new_findings.topic: Instagram DM outreach volume and rate-limit management
new_findings.title: Send 50 IG DMs/day; back off 5 below rate-limit trigger

variants.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
variants.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

sources.quote: I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message
For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented daily volume target, rate-limit protocol, or follow-up cadence (§6 GAP). No internal baseline on outreach message structure or follow-up timing exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a daily DM volume SOP: start at 50/day, instrument the rate-limit floor, and enforce a 24-hour → voice-message follow-up sequence. Document this in the acquisition runbook so it can be handed to a future acquisition VA.

new_findings.topic: Social content strategy — filler vs. vertical content split
new_findings.title: Two-content-type IG framework: 'filler' (lifestyle) keeps algorithm happy; 'vertical' (viral-engineered) drives new traffic

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

sources.quote: Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process
Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature 8-step inspo-driven pipeline that uses viral data to decide what creators film (§1), which directly parallels the competitor analysis piece here. However, Palm's posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP). The filler vs. vertical content taxonomy is not explicitly documented in Palm's system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formally adopt the filler/vertical taxonomy inside Palm's content pipeline. Tag each Asset in Airtable as 'filler' or 'vertical/traffic-driver' so editors and schedulers can enforce the 2–3/day cadence with the right mix. This codifies the cadence GAP noted in §1.

new_findings.title: Let AI sort and convert new subs; route spenders to human chatters

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

variants.claim: AI voice cloning and AI-assisted chat monetization can be sold as premium products and scale fan monetization without extra creator effort.
variants.what_they_do: Uses AI voice cloning for chat voice notes that are reportedly indistinguishable from the real creator, sells custom AI voice notes for ~100 dollars each at volume, and uses location/timezone-based priority mass-messaging (auto-unsend if no reply within an hour) to catch high-value fans at active times.

sources.quote: I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients
We do AI voice cloning... selling AI voice notes for $100 and we're selling a lot of them
priority messaging based on location... if they do not respond within an hour, the message will be unsent

palm_comparison.vs_us: Defines the target architecture for Palm's AI-chatting project and future in-house team (§8 build-toward): AI for volume/sorting, humans for whale retention.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design Palm's chat roadmap as a hybrid: AI for new-sub volume and spender-sorting, human chatters owning small VIP client lists for retention, with unlock-rate as a tracked KPI.

new_findings.title: AI personas remove model-dependency risk and keep 100% of revenue

variants.claim: Operators argue an owned synthetic creator beats managing real talent because it removes model-dependency, agency-hopping and split-revenue risk while keeping all earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Frames traditional OFM as a 'casino' (lazy models, theft, unpaid invoices, agency hoppers) where a $24k month nets only ~$6.5k after split; an AI model on Fanvue keeps 100% (minus 20% platform fee) for ~$17.5k net, fully owner-controlled and self-reliant.

variants.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.

sources.quote: you get 100% share obviously with this business model
It's a casino because that's basically what it is
they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs real creators on OnlyFans today with revenue split (§0) and treats AI personas as a future scaling lever (§3 MATURE). Palm's AI personas still funnel to OnlyFans via social, unlike the AI-creator/Fanvue model taught here.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold for the future AI-creator line: validate the owner-keeps-100% Fanvue economics as the business case for a Palm AI line, separate from the real-creator OnlyFans core.

new_findings.topic: Tiered commission escalation to reduce signing friction
new_findings.title: Start at 20-30% commission and escalate by milestone to earn the full split

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

variants.claim: Deal structure should flex to the creator's revenue tier instead of applying a single 50/50 split across all creators.
variants.what_they_do: Uses tiered economics: beginners via a paid coaching/productized model (not 50/50), mid-tier (10-50k/mo) on 50/50 plus an upfront setup fee, and whales (100k+/mo) on a lower rev-share plus a fixed monthly retainer (e.g. 20k/mo retainer + 50% of upside above current revenue) so the agency is profitable from month one.

sources.quote: my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward
you have to have tiers... Your deal structure has to flex to the tier
I'm going to charge you 20K a month retainer... I only want 50% of what we make you above that 100K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % at onboarding as a fixed field (§7), but there is no documented tiered/milestone-based commission model or floor-guarantee tactic for established creators. No internal baseline exists for how Palm's splits are structured or justified. This is a §7 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a documented commission framework with two tracks: (1) New creator / unproven account — start at 30-35%, escalate to 50% upon hitting defined monthly revenue threshold (suggest $10k/month, consistent with the 3-month target the speakers reference). (2) Established creator already earning — offer a monthly floor guarantee equal to their current earnings; charge full split only on revenue above that floor in month one. Codify both in the onboarding contract wizard.

new_findings.title: Salary creator model: pay creator fixed rate, agency keeps ~90% of revenue

variants.claim: In a vertical-scale agency, paying creators a fixed monthly salary (e.g. $2,500–$10,000) regardless of page revenue allows the agency to retain ~90% of profits.
variants.what_they_do: Agency signs creators on a flat monthly salary tied to cost-of-living (e.g. $2,500–$10,000/mo depending on location). If the page earns $100K/mo, the agency keeps ~$90K. Creator has no performance incentive; agency owns all marketing decisions. This is contrasted with rev-share models where creator keeps 70%+ and agency takes ~30%.

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: I could theoretically be paying this creator $2500 every single month, depending on where she lives, upwards to $10,000 a month. I keep 90% of the profit.
he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses a commission/revenue-split model set per creator at onboarding (§7) — no documented salary-creator option. Palm's split rationale is not written down (§7 GAP). The salary model is a materially different deal structure Palm has not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'salary creator' tier to Palm's deal structure documentation (§7). Define eligibility criteria (e.g. creator has no existing following; Palm does all marketing) and salary bands by geography. This model makes most sense for Palm's AI personas or for raw-talent real creators with no existing audience, where Palm controls 100% of the funnel.

new_findings.title: Use 'what breaks if revenue doubles tomorrow' as the primary systems-gap diagnostic

variants.claim: The most practical way to identify scaling bottlenecks is to ask 'what would break if our revenue doubled overnight' rather than asking 'how do we grow.'
variants.what_they_do: TDM frames the scaling question as a stress-test thought experiment: list every department and workflow, then identify which ones would snap under 2x load. The output is a prioritized list of systems, hires, and automations to build before growth demands them, not after.

variants.claim: Agencies scale most effectively by diagnosing the single largest bottleneck — social coverage, chatter quality, chatter hours — and solving only that before moving to the next.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan walks through a sequential bottleneck-resolution framework: (1) Could more social accounts drive more revenue? Yes → outsource social. (2) Could 24/7 chatting earn more? Yes → hire chatters. (3) Could better-trained chatters earn more? Yes → build chatter training. Each step is solved fully before the next is attempted. He explicitly warns against trying to solve all problems simultaneously, which leads to overwhelm and paralysis.

sources.quote: what would happen and what would break if revenue doubled tomorrow

look at where your bottlenecks are in your business and work from there so you know could you make more money with a client if you had more social media accounts running traffic yes okay well how do you do that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is in an active SMM hub redesign (§11) and has several in-flight initiatives. There is no documented stress-test exercise or pre-mortem for scaling. Given that Palm is building infrastructure now, this lens is immediately applicable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Conduct a 'double revenue' war-game session: map every current operational workflow and ask what would break at 2x creator count or 2x content volume. Use the output to sequence the remaining build plan — prioritize fixes to workflows that would break first (likely: content pipeline throughput, Amin/posting bottleneck, creator onboarding, and the chat team handoff).

new_findings.title: Daily feedback cadence to accelerate employee ramp 7x vs weekly

variants.claim: Giving employees feedback every single day instead of once a week makes them progress seven times faster, cutting ramp time from seven months to thirty days.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or a designated lead delivers individual feedback to every employee daily — not a team standup, but specific corrective or reinforcing input tied to that person's KPIs and tasks. This is paired with formal performance reviews on a recurring schedule.

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to record written takeaways at the end of every shift — specifically one actionable improvement for the next shift — accelerates skill development during the first month.
variants.what_they_do: For roughly the first month, each chatter writes a brief end-of-shift note answering: 'What are the actionable steps I'll take next shift to improve on what I did this shift?' This creates a self-directed feedback loop between supervised live-training sessions.

sources.quote: give your employees feedback once a week, I give them feedback every single day. That means my employees are going to progress seven times faster
For the first month or so, get the chatters to record their daily takeaways. At the end of each shift, okay, what are the actionable steps you're going to take next shift

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented feedback cadence for editors, Amin, or any contractor (§10 — THIN). There are no performance review cycles or KPI dashboards per employee in the codebase. The closest analogue is the editor task queue in the portal, which surfaces output but not a feedback loop.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a lightweight daily async check-in for each active role (editor, posting contractor, eventually chatters): a shared Telegram thread or Airtable comment thread where the reviewer flags one specific 'fix this' and one 'good job' per day per person. Formalise this before hiring the in-house chat team (~1 yr roadmap item, §8).

new_findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out
focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

new_findings.title: Score every creator on gross profit, team hours, and operational drag

variants.claim: A creator profitability scorecard — covering direct costs, team hours, founder attention, growth potential, and content consistency — is the core tool that separates good business decisions from emotional guesses.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator Dylan tracks: gross revenue, agency net, direct costs (chat team, editor/VA, software, promo), gross profit, team hours required, founder hours required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag. He illustrates that a creator doing $12k gross with $1k costs and zero management friction (producing $5k profit) can be more valuable than a $20k creator costing $4k in fulfillment (producing $6k profit) but consuming disproportionate attention. The scorecard feeds an A/B/C creator classification used to allocate resources and make cut decisions.

variants.claim: A simple four-part operating system — weekly scorecard, creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and founder replacement ladder — is sufficient to escape the $100k/month trap without building a bloated corporate structure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's weekly scorecard covers four domains: (1) Revenue: creator gross, agency net, gross profit, owner profit, revenue per creator, top/declining performers, content compliance; (2) Acquisition: outreach sent, replies, qualified leads, booked calls, shows, closes, new creators signed; (3) Delivery: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator complaints; (4) Team: who hit targets, who missed, which bottleneck repeated, what was fixed. The delivery operating system specifically needs 7 pieces: weekly creator review, content output tracker, chat performance tracker, revenue breakdown, issues/bottleneck list, next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action. The founder replacement ladder moves any recurring founder task through: do it → document it → delegate it → review the output → metrics manage it → someone else fully owns it.

sources.quote: this is why you need a creator profitability scorecard. For every creator, the score is gross revenue, agency net, direct costs, gross profit, team hours required, founder involvement required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag

Creator B does 12K gross. You keep 6K, but she's easy to manage. She sends content like a clockwork. She barely touches your attention. And the team cost against her is only 1K. So she produces 5K in profit.
you literally just need a very simple minimum viable operating system with four parts. A weekly scorecard, a creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and the founder replacement ladder

A simple delivery operating system needs seven pieces. A weekly creator review, a content output tracker, a chat performance tracker, a revenue breakdown, an issues and bottleneck list, and next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action.
you can't scale what you only feel. You scale what you can see every week.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM on the fan side (§9), and invoicing computes agency net per creator (§7), but there is no per-creator profitability scorecard that includes direct fulfillment costs and operational drag. This is an explicit GAP (§7). The Airtable `Creators` table exists but is not instrumented for margin tracking.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Profitability' view to the admin portal (or Airtable `Creators` table) with fields: gross revenue, agency net, estimated fulfillment cost (chat allocation + editor hours + VA hours + tools), gross profit, estimated team hours/week, estimated founder touch/week, ABC rating, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Run the audit monthly. This directly addresses Palm's §7 GAP and feeds the retention/churn work (§9).

new_findings.title: Classify every creator A/B/C and allocate resources accordingly — cut C creators

variants.claim: Most agencies carry too many low-quality ('C') creators out of fear of losing gross revenue, which silently caps growth by stealing attention from better creators.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan classifies creators into three tiers: A = high upside, consistent content, coachable, scalable brand; B = potential, needs structure, worth developing; C = inconsistent, ignores feedback, creates chaos, low upside, drains team. A creators get premium attention and resources. B creators get active development. C creators get fixed, downgraded, or removed. The diagnostic question is: 'Knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today?' If no, there must be a reason she is still consuming company resources. He notes a 30k/month agency with 2 great creators beats a 100k/month agency with 14 nightmares.

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

sources.quote: the creator audit is where you classify your roster into A, B, and C
knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today? That is the last question. That's very important.
most agencies carry way too many Creators, and they keep them out of the fear of losing their revenue number
Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a `Creators` table with status tracking and onboarding workflow (§6), but no documented creator-tier classification, no systematic review cadence, and no written policy on when to downgrade or drop a creator. This is a GAP (§9 retention, §7 pricing/deal structure). Palm's creator acquisition is currently reactive/Josh-DM-led (§6) with no quality filter documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a quarterly creator audit in the admin portal: list all active creators, assign A/B/C rating based on gross profit, content compliance rate, team hours consumed, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Set explicit policies: A creators get priority chat team allocation and content pipeline slots; B creators get a 60-day improvement plan; C creators get a downgrade notice or off-boarding. Pair with the profitability scorecard (finding above) to make ratings data-driven rather than subjective.

new_findings.title: Cold outreach is a volume-consistency-conversation game

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

sources.quote: the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text
after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led but not run as a tracked, committed-volume system (§6 THIN); no defined daily quota or multi-account structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize Palm outbound as a committed daily-volume system (per-day DM quota, multi-account, voice/video openers, qualify-in-conversation) rather than ad-hoc DMs.

new_findings.topic: AI reel animation — 'secret prompt' technique for virality
new_findings.title: Extract the core action from a viral reel as the animation prompt to replicate its hook

variants.claim: The key to making an AI-animated reel go viral is identifying the specific physical action in the original viral reel and using that exact action as the animation prompt, rather than a generic description.
variants.what_they_do: When a viral reel is identified (e.g., 1M views), the operator distills the core action ('girl is doing a backflip') as the animation prompt fed into the AI tool (Kling). They then ask the AI tool to refine and make the prompt 'as realistic as possible.' The first frame of the recreated image is animated with this action prompt.

variants.claim: A fully AI-generated reel (no face swap, no real footage) avoids copyright and brand risk while still achieving viral reach when built from reverse-engineered prompts of high-performing inspo reels.
variants.what_they_do: Operator (1) identifies high-performing AI content on IG (targeting 100k–1M view reels, motion reels preferred), (2) screenshots the best frame, (3) feeds screenshot to Grok or ChatGPT to generate a detailed image prompt, (4) pastes prompt into Kling/MidJourney/CR to generate the image, (5) animates it in Kling, (6) adds captions in CapCut, (7) posts one reel/day for 10 days. At 10 posts they analyze which performed best and scale that template with a 'trial reel strategy' (20 reels/day).

sources.quote: the secret prompt is what the original reel is doing to make it viral… if the girl is doing a backflip the secret prompt is going to be the girl is doing a backflip
ask the deep fake to refine the prompt and make it as much realistic as possible
screenshot and ask Grok or ChatGPT to make a detailed prompt of this image and with the prompt you're going to paste it on Kling, MidJourney, or CR
post one reel a day for 10 days… you're going to analyze the data for 10 days… see which one performs the best

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's §1 pipeline includes AI vision analysis of inspo reels (transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score) but the output is a filming idea for a real creator, not an animation prompt for synthetic video. This technique is specifically for AI content production and has no current Palm equivalent. The underlying logic — isolate the hook action, not just the aesthetic — is relevant to Palm's brief-writing for real creators too.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona build-toward (§3/§0): incorporate 'action extraction prompt' as a named step in the synthetic reel SOP — after screenshotting inspo, explicitly ask GPT 'what is the specific physical action happening in this reel?' and use that answer as the primary animation prompt. For real creators: consider adding 'primary hook action' as a structured field in the inspo analysis output (§1, step 3) to sharpen filming briefs.

new_findings.title: Done beats perfect; minimize record-to-publish friction

variants.claim: Reducing friction between idea and published content matters more than production polish, because perfection kills the frequency that actually compounds.
variants.what_they_do: Films unedited YouTube videos from bullet points with just a mic and DJI Osmo + Screenflow, uses 4-5 templated thumbnails, and delegates only short-form editing; films an idea the moment it strikes rather than deferring it.

variants.claim: Brand equity decays with every silent day, so consistent daily multi-post output across platforms is required maintenance, not optional.
variants.what_they_do: Posts 6x/day across IG and TikTok personally; frames missed days as compound interest in reverse where creators, algorithms, and the market all forget; only tracks posting consistency (plus inbounds, repeat viewers, proof) as a real KPI, ignoring vanity metrics.

sources.quote: perfection is what kills frequency. So done always beats perfect
I just like to reduce the friction between me having an idea and then making content
I post six times a day
every time that you stop posting for a week, your brand equity decays

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's editor workflow is heavy and approval-gated (§1: 2 slots/day per creator, revisions) — appropriate for monetizable social assets but slow for founder/brand content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any Palm/founder brand content, run a low-friction lane (bullet points, templated thumbnails, minimal editing) separate from the polished creator-asset pipeline.

new_findings.title: A cheap competitive model wins on marketing, not on max realism

variants.claim: Operators stress that earnings come from marketing, niching and sales rather than from having the most photorealistic model, and a sub-$100 toolchain is enough to compete.
variants.what_they_do: An 'intermediate' workflow (base model + reference set + ChatGPT-generated prompt bank + skin-enhancer for pores/flaws, under $100) is presented as the banker that made five figures; advanced ComfyUI/outsourced-developer models are optional for those with cash, but 'it isn't the quality of your model, it's the marketing, the sales.'

sources.quote: this whole workflow you can set up for under $100
It isn't the quality of your model. It's the marketing, the sales

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's strength is data-driven content ideation (§1 MATURE) and a hardware-isolated AI-persona program (§3); model-generation toolchain economics are relevant only to the future AI line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the future AI line, budget a lean generation toolchain and weight investment toward marketing/niching over chasing maximal realism; keep ComfyUI/dev-grade models for flagship personas only.

new_findings.title: AI-image watermarks (SynthID) are the new ban trigger; switch generators

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

sources.quote: Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI program is EU AI Act compliant with required AI label + watermark (§3), the opposite stance; for real creators Palm has no documented platform-risk/ban-avoidance policy (§4/§5 gaps).
palm_comparison.recommendation: AI line: note label/watermark compliance is in direct tension with the ban-evasion tactics gurus use, so plan for platform-compliant distribution. Real creators: adopt the human-looking-bio + safe link-provider hygiene now.

new_findings.title: Coach creators on 4 non-negotiable quality standards: lighting, audio, background, and variety

variants.claim: Agencies should proactively coach creators on production quality during onboarding rather than waiting for bad content to arrive and damage relationships.
variants.what_they_do: They explicitly advise creators on: (1) lighting — minimum ring light or studio light for long-form video, softer/natural for sexting sets to avoid an overly-produced look; (2) audio — no background noise; (3) background — clean, uncluttered; (4) variety — multiple outfits, angles, lingerie styles, and outdoor locations. This is delivered as part of the onboarding process, not as reactive feedback.

sources.quote: lighting is probably the number one most important thing when it comes to taking high quality content make sure that you're using at least a ring light
make sure that the background of the videos and the pictures and everything doesn't have a bunch like a mess or Garbage

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) evaluates *incoming* inspo reels for viral score but there is no documented creator-facing quality brief or production standards SOP. Quality feedback is presumably reactive via the revision workflow (Tasks table).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Content Quality Standards' PDF/Notion doc covering the four pillars above. Send it during onboarding and reference it in weekly content briefs. Add a quality-gate field to the Assets Airtable table so editors can flag substandard submissions for coaching rather than silently fixing or discarding.

new_findings.title: On-shift group call + peer-authority system accelerates collective chatter improvement

variants.claim: An open call for all chatters currently on shift — combined with assigning subject-matter 'authorities' among chatters — creates collaborative learning that makes the whole team improve faster than top-down coaching alone.
variants.what_they_do: Maintain an always-open call channel for chatters on the same shift so they can ask each other for help and share mistakes in real time. Identify each chatter's main strength (e.g., scripting, PPV captioning). When another chatter needs help in that area, route them to the peer expert rather than the manager. This builds internal figures of authority and reduces manager bottleneck.

sources.quote: having an on shift call where the chatters who are currently working can talk to each other
Chatters tend to have one main strength. Identify what that is and then whenever a different chatter asks for help on that topic, you send them to that chatter

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat team structure at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team. Relevant also to AI chatting project's team design.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When standing up the in-house chat team, create a Telegram or Discord group call as an 'on-shift room.' Map each chatter's strongest skill in Airtable and use that routing table to build peer mentorship from week one.

new_findings.topic: Inbound-pull DM strategy vs. mass PPV blasting
new_findings.title: Use feed posts as DM bait to pull fans inbound rather than blasting PPV

variants.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
variants.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.

sources.quote: post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented DM-pull strategy or mass-message cadence guidance (§8 outsourced, §5 GAP on traffic/distribution strategy). This inbound-pull framing is a complementary tactic to whatever the external chat team is doing and is relevant for the in-house roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate 'availability bait' posts as a scheduled content type in the Publer/Airtable pipeline — short captions designed to trigger DM inflow, not tied to a content asset. Brief the external chat team on this so they're primed when the flood comes, and document it as a standard practice for the in-house chat SOP.

new_findings.title: Use Reddit profile bio as the single OF conversion point; layer IG or Twitter as intermediate funnel where direct OF links are banned

variants.claim: 99% of NSFW subreddits prohibit OF links in posts but allow them in the Reddit profile bio, making profile visits the core conversion mechanic; where even bio links are banned, route traffic through Twitter or Instagram as an intermediate step.
variants.what_they_do: Standard setup: OF link in Reddit profile bio only (not in post body or comments). Post content drives profile clicks; profile bio converts to OF. Where subreddits ban OF bio links (e.g. after saturation): place IG or Twitter link in bio instead, grow those accounts as a secondary funnel, then convert to OF from there. This also unlocks access to additional subreddits that block OF links but permit social links. Reddit DMs used sparingly as a tertiary funnel (limited by Reddit ban risk on high-volume DM sending).

sources.quote: basically 99% of the subreddits allows you to post it on your profile on your Reddit profile in the bio. That's it.
instead of getting all these fans and getting to getting them to the only fans straight away… you could also say, okay, I want to funnel them to, for example, an Instagram account

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit funnel documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG accounts and has Publer scheduling (§5), so the IG intermediate-funnel variant aligns with existing infrastructure. The Twitter/X layer is absent from Palm's current stack.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When testing Reddit, default to OF link in bio as the primary CTA. Before launching a creator on Reddit, audit which target subreddits permit OF bio links vs. only social links, and set up the appropriate intermediate account (IG preferred, as Palm already operates IG). Track Reddit → IG → OF conversion separately from direct Reddit → OF to measure intermediate-funnel efficiency.

new_findings.topic: Reddit minimum viable setup for new agencies
new_findings.title: Start Reddit with one VA, one phone, one account per creator — no scheduling tools, no black hat

variants.claim: Any agency new to Reddit should start with the simplest possible setup — a dedicated VA with a phone-based account posting organically to well-matched subreddits — before investing in black-hat infrastructure, because even this baseline can generate 10+ paid subs/day.
variants.what_they_do: Expert's explicit minimum recommendation regardless of agency size: assign one VA per creator for Reddit, have them use a physical phone (not desktop — Reddit deprioritises desktop activity), post organically every day or every other day to keep the account warm, no scheduling software, no automation, no upvote manipulation. Find the right subreddits through manual trial first. This single-account approach can yield 10+ paid subs/day on a well-matched creator with hundreds of viable organic NSFW subreddits available.

sources.quote: get a dedicated VA. Not no scheduling, no post scheduling, nothing. If you're new to Reddit, get a dedicated VA… just posts every day in or every other day to keep the account warm
one account could definitely get you, let's say, anywhere upwards from 10 paid subs per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently uses Publer for scheduling (§5) and Telegram routing to Amin for manual posting. The phone-native, no-scheduler Reddit requirement is architecturally different. Palm has no VA role defined for Reddit posting (§10 GAP — org structure thin). This is the lowest-risk entry point for Palm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm pilots Reddit for even one creator, assign a VA (or redirect Amin) to phone-based manual posting. Do not route Reddit through Publer — Reddit flags scheduler signatures. Allocate 30 days of organic posting to 20–50 well-matched NSFW subreddits before evaluating whether black-hat investment is warranted. Track subs attributed to Reddit via UTM or unique landing page.

new_findings.title: Automate X/Twitter R4R via Xbox bot + antidetect browser for passive low-effort traffic

variants.claim: Twitter R4R (retweet-for-retweet) automated via the Xbox bot is the easiest traffic source for beginners and runs 24/7 with minimal setup, though it delivers lower-quality fans than Reddit.
variants.what_they_do: Create X account, warm for 24 hours, purchase X Premium, join retweet groups, set up antidetect browser, install Xbox bot. Make one post, pin it, start the bot — it handles R4R continuously. The agency positions this as a side channel, not primary, due to lower subscriber quality.

sources.quote: Twitter, all we do is just retweet for retweet using Xbox… the bot does the rest for you. Does route 24/7
Twitter isn't the highest quality, but it is the easiest. So it's definitely good to do on the side

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal Twitter/X traffic strategy documented at Palm (§5 🔲 GAP). This is a zero-marginal-cost automation layer Palm could layer on top of existing content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Test X R4R as a low-priority side channel for 2–3 real creators: one account each, Xbox bot + antidetect browser, run for 30 days and measure OF click-throughs via unique tracking links. If CTR justifies the account management overhead, systematize. Keep expectation low — use as supplementary, not primary.

new_findings.topic: Virality pattern recognition as a learnable skill
new_findings.title: Train agency operators to diagnose WHY specific video moments drive views/comments

variants.claim: Learning to identify the precise moment in a video responsible for views or comments — not just copying formats — is the analytical skill that scales organic revenue past $10K/mo.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin reviews videos with operators on calls and annotates specific timestamps: 'this point in the video is why it got views; this point is why it got comments.' Over 4–6 months of repeated exposure, operators internalize the pattern and can evaluate new content without needing external input. It is described as time + experience compressed through deliberate coaching.

sources.quote: every single one of our calls I would try and underline exactly what made a video go viral like why some videos worked why some didn't
you can look at a video and be like this point in the video is why I got so many views or this point in the video is why I got so many comments

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) uses AI vision analysis to score viral potential (step 3: transcript, visual breakdown, hook, viral score). This is automated signal — but the human analytical skill of diagnosing WHY a specific moment works is not documented as a training output for Palm staff. Palm has the data layer; the interpretive training layer is a gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'virality annotation' training protocol for Palm editors and content managers: when reviewing top-performing reels, require annotators to tag the exact frame/moment that drove the metric and the mechanism (hook, pattern interrupt, comment bait, etc.). Accumulate these as a labeled training set both for future AI scoring refinement and for human operator skill-building.

new_findings.topic: Traffic continuity as model retention lever
new_findings.title: Treat consistent traffic delivery as the #1 model retention mechanism

variants.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
variants.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.

sources.quote: Models are leaving, your revenue is going down... we've been able to keep all of our models, even get referrals, whilst most agencies are losing their creators
you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy (§9 🔲 GAP) and no multi-channel traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP). If Palm's real creators are currently IG-dependent for traffic, they face the same churn risk described here. The connection between traffic reliability and model retention is not captured in Palm's current framework.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'traffic continuity during IG downtime' as an explicit SLA in creator agreements and as a tracked KPI in the whale-hunting / fan-tracker dashboards (§9). Build the narrative internally: Palm's job is to deliver traffic regardless of platform conditions. Use this framing in creator acquisition pitches (§6) as a differentiator vs. single-channel agencies.

new_findings.title: 30-day written warning for bottom-20% chatters; immediate fire for worst performers

variants.claim: After scoring chatters, bottom 20% should receive either an immediate termination (truly poor) or a formal 30-day written warning with a clear performance improvement expectation.
variants.what_they_do: After building the leaderboard: worst performers are fired immediately. Bottom 20% who show potential receive a written warning stating explicitly that they have 30 days to improve or they will be replaced. No ambiguity — the timeline and consequence are stated upfront.

sources.quote: take the 20% who are either going to get a written warning or you're just going to let them go
You have 30 days. Get your shit together

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chatter management process (§8, outsourced). For the in-house roadmap, this is a concrete PIP (Performance Improvement Plan) template to adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Draft a written warning template for chatter PIPs before scaling the in-house team. Include the 5 KPI scores, the target thresholds, the 30-day deadline, and the consequence. Pair it with a termination checklist so the process is consistent regardless of who manages it.

new_findings.title: PPV Anchors: Plant a story or fantasy 6–24 hours before sending the PPV to manufacture perceived relevance

variants.claim: Setting up a PPV in advance — by mentioning a dream, fantasy, new outfit, or story — gives chatters a credible, personalized reason for sending the video later, making it seem far more valuable than a cold send.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter plants an anchor in DMs (e.g. 'I just had the craziest dream about doing XYZ with you in my green lingerie') or on the feed (e.g. 'just got this new outfit, what do you think?' with a photo). They do NOT sell immediately. They return 6, 12, or 24 hours later referencing the anchor ('I couldn't help but make that dream a reality — I put on that green lingerie and…') and send the PPV. The delay is what makes it feel authentic rather than scripted. Feed-level anchors (outfit post, emotional story post) can hit many subs simultaneously, with chatters following up individually in DMs.

sources.quote: we can set up pay-per-views in advanced in the DMs or on the main feed... this should act as lore for the pay-per-view, which gives you a reason for sending it later
that delay between when you bring up the anchor and when you go into sell it is what makes it seem authentic

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal SOP for pre-seeding PPV context exists (§8 outsourced, §2 thin on PPV vault strategy). Palm does have a content posting pipeline (§1) and scheduling infrastructure (§5), which could be used to coordinate feed-level anchors with chatter follow-up timing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 'PPV anchor' protocol into the future in-house chat SOP: define anchor types (DM-personal vs feed-broadcast), specify the delay window (6–24 hrs), and create a coordination mechanism between the posting schedule (Publer/Telegram) and the chat team so chatters know when an anchor post goes live and can follow up.
