This is a high-leverage tactic because it uses existing content infrastructure.

new_findings.topic: Indirect sexting as PPV pitch mechanism
new_findings.title: Pitch PPVs via 'indirect sexting' — escalate a fantasy tangent, then link the PPV as the physical manifestation

variants.claim: Rather than describing the video's explicit content directly (which sounds repetitive and generic), chatters sext about an unrelated fantasy the creator 'has been having,' escalate it fully, then link the PPV as what the creator made while thinking about that fantasy.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter chooses a fantasy (threesome, free use, femdom, cheating, etc.) — ideally one the subscriber has shown interest in — and opens with 'there's something I've been thinking about but I'm embarrassed to say.' They build out the fantasy in full descriptive detail, getting the sub emotionally invested. When the sub is clearly engaged, the chatter pitches the PPV: 'I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time.' They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.

sources.quote: an alternative to that is sexting as if you were opening up about a fantasy that the creator has been having
you keep talking about the fantasy until they're clearly into it, and then you link that to the next pay-per-view saying something like, I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatting scripts or sexting frameworks exist at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is a build-toward tactic for the in-house chat team and AI chatting project. The AI chatting project in particular would need this fantasy-ladder logic codified as a sequencing rule.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the in-house chat roadmap, build a 'fantasy library' per creator (ranked low-to-high explicitness) that chatters and eventually AI chatters draw from sequentially per subscriber. Document the indirect sexting structure as a script template: embarrassed open → full fantasy build → PPV link with timestamped personal connection. This is especially relevant for the AI chatting project where consistent, structured dialogue trees are required.

new_findings.title: Optimize acquisition for ROI/LTV, not cost-per-lead

variants.claim: Acquisition should be optimized on return-on-spend against creator lifetime value, not cost-per-lead, which lets you profitably pay far more for high-quality leads.
variants.what_they_do: Tracks average creator retention and revenue to derive LTV; with a 5k LTV avatar, a 500 dollar cost-per-lead beats a 100 dollar lead with low LTV. Once 100+ avatar leads are captured, builds 1% lookalike audiences to compound quality.

sources.quote: the focus metric always is ROI... cost per lead is a bad metric and doesn't really mean anything
have the balls to do that if you know that the average LTV is 5K, 10K, 20K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm computes weekly invoices but has no documented LTV, retention-rate, or acquisition unit-economics tracking (§7 THIN, §9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Instrument creator LTV (avg tenure x avg monthly Palm revenue) and retention so any future paid acquisition is judged on ROI, and so the data feeds pricing and retention decisions too.

new_findings.title: Prefer percentage deals with Western creators over LATAM salary models

variants.claim: Percentage splits with US/UK/Australian creators outperform paying monthly salaries to lower-cost-region creators because Western audiences spend more and the model carries no upfront cost.
variants.what_they_do: Moved off salaried Colombian/Argentinian models (high overhead, hard to reach US spenders, recurring raise pressure) to zero-upfront percentage deals (e.g. 50/50) with US/UK/AU creators whose audiences actually pay premium PPV prices.

sources.quote: going from zero to 10K with a salary model is like a billion times harder than with a percentage model from the west
If it's a 50/50, it's pretty much based on what you make her. She takes half

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission per-creator at onboarding but has no written split/model rationale (§7 GAP); this benchmarks Palm's percentage approach against the salary alternative.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's percentage-over-salary rationale and Western-creator targeting as the codified pricing/commission position.

new_findings.title: Automate fan re-engagement so chatters focus only on inbound

variants.claim: Revenue scales when automated, rotating 'smart message' sequences trigger on fan activity to remove human error and free chatters to work only inbound conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up Infloww smart-message 'nudge' collections that fire when a fan comes online (after a delay), rotates ~10 collections every two weeks so messages feel fresh, auto-unsends ignored sequences, and stops the sequence once the fan replies—so chatters never do manual outreach.

sources.quote: you want to completely remove human error from chatters
this feature has made me hundreds of thousands of dollars for all my models

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's tech spine has Publer scheduling but no fan-CRM-driven re-engagement automation tied to chatting (§8/§9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Plan a smart-message-style automated re-engagement layer (activity-triggered, rotating, auto-unsend) for Palm's future fan CRM / AI-chat stack.

new_findings.title: Spin up backup/secondary accounts and post across many platforms

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution strategy (Reddit, multi-account, S4S) is an explicit GAP (§5); cadence is partially uncodified (§1). Note: this overlaps Palm's AI warmup multi-account capability (§3/§4).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-account/multi-platform traffic strategy with tracking links for attribution and a week-ahead scheduling cadence; reuse AI-warmup account infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Weigh AI's zero-follower cold start and believability problem before betting on it

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs hardware-isolated AI warmup + EU AI Act labeling (§3/§4), which directly mitigates the cold-start/believability risk this raises; useful as a counter-view weighting Palm's real-first core (§0).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep real creators as the core and treat AI as a hedge; lean on Palm's warmup/labeling stack to de-risk the cold-start and believability problems this guru flags before scaling an AI line.

new_findings.title: Steer on CAC:LTV and margin, not followers

variants.claim: Allocating resources by cost-per-acquisition versus LTV and target margin, rather than by follower counts or a raw LTV number, is how a scaled agency decides where to invest.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media tracks free and paid LTV over 7 and 30 days against a roughly $1 Reddit CAC, targets a 50% margin on base traffic (accepting 20-30% on low-effort purchased traffic), and color-codes models so investment flows to the highest CAC:LTV returns; the founder calls 'what is a good LTV' the wrong question.

sources.quote: we are always monitoring free LTV and paid LTV over a 7-day and 30-day period
what is a good LTV... it's just the wrong question... what we actually care about is cac to ltv

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data but leadership reporting is in-flight and there is no standard CAC:LTV or margin-per-creator view (§9 PARTIAL, §7 THIN). Gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a per-creator CAC:LTV dashboard with a target margin, and allocate posting/acquisition spend by that ratio instead of by follower vanity metrics.

new_findings.topic: VA-staffed repurposing operation — scaling social output without creator time
new_findings.title: Staff a separate VA team to run repurposing accounts, freeing creators for original filming

variants.claim: Repurposing accounts are an operational task fully separable from the creator, enabling unlimited horizontal account scaling via VAs without increasing creator workload.
variants.what_they_do: Once repurposing accounts are set up (distinct identities, rotation rules), the agency hires VAs whose sole job is to download, structurally re-edit, and post content to the repurposing account cluster. The creator only films for the organic primary accounts. This decouples content volume from creator availability.

sources.quote: you can still have your main accounts, your main organic accounts, pull all the views, but you can hire 50 VAs and repost on as many accounts as you like on this side

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently uses Amin (contractor) for manual posting (§5) and editors for production (§1). There is no VA layer for repurposing account management. Hiring/delegation infrastructure for this role does not exist yet (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm is ready to scale social distribution, define a 'Repurposing VA' role with a clear SOP: download source, apply structural edits per checklist, assign to rotation slot, schedule via Publer. Start with one VA per 5 repurposing accounts. Document the SOP before hiring.

new_findings.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) so this loop is bypassed at the current stage. However, Palm has no documented chatter comp model, hiring criteria, or onboarding process for when it builds an in-house team. This is a build-toward gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin documenting chatter comp benchmarks now (flat rate ranges, commission % norms, hybrid structures) sourced from competitor content like this. Build a chatter job description and scoring rubric ahead of the in-house chat team launch. This prevents scrambling when the timeline moves up.

new_findings.title: Package VA chatter training as a zipped module with persona PDF

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

sources.quote: some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8) with no documented internal SOP or onboarding material. There is no equivalent persona brief or chatter training kit. This is a direct gap to fill as Palm builds toward an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), create a per-creator ZIP/Notion training module template: persona card (age, niche, tone, emojis, pet names), platform walkthrough, PPV pricing ladder, and a baseline script. Standardize this as the chatter onboarding artifact.

new_findings.title: Never send multiple follow-up messages to a non-responding subscriber

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat SOP (§8 outsourced). If the external team lacks this discipline it may be causing churn Palm can't see. This rule should be a core constraint in both the in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt guardrails.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a hard rule to the chatter SOP and AI chatting system prompt: after one unanswered message, no further text messages until the subscriber replies (only the sad-face emoji exception is permitted). Make this a QA audit criterion when reviewing chatter logs.

new_findings.title: Position the agency offer around creator outcomes (time saved, revenue growth), not the 50% split

variants.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
variants.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.

sources.quote: what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission model or offer framing (§7 GAP). Commission % is set per-creator at onboarding with no stated rationale or tiering. This is Palm's #1 identified gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write Palm's standard offer narrative: lead with the two pain points (fan growth, time), show a concrete growth trajectory from an existing client, and frame the 50% split as a performance-aligned partnership rather than a fee. Consider creating a one-page 'offer doc' with revenue-growth evidence that can be shared during acquisition outreach.

new_findings.title: Never sell PPV packs or bundles — sell individual pieces only

variants.claim: Selling content in packs commoditizes it and undermines the per-item price anchoring that drives high revenue per fan.
variants.what_they_do: The agency explicitly bans 'packs' as a pricing format. All PPV is sold as individual items at or above the price floors. The reasoning is that packs reframe the value proposition toward volume/discount, which is the same psychological trap as low per-item pricing.

sources.quote: you especially don't want to sell packs no packs

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented stance on packs vs. individual PPV pricing (§7 GAP). No internal policy exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'no packs' rule to the Palm pricing policy alongside the price floors. Instruct the external chat team accordingly. When building the in-house chat SOP, codify individual-item-only PPV selling as a default.

new_findings.title: Run 1–30 on-brand comment baits per day per Threads account to boost engagement signals

variants.claim: Strategic comment baiting on Threads boosts engagement signals, but must look human and on-brand to avoid spam detection.
variants.what_they_do: TDM plants 1–10 comment baits per day per account (capped at 25–30 max) — posts or replies designed to provoke comments. They require the bait to be niche-relevant, human-sounding, and directly related to the original post to avoid spam flags. Volume is scaled based on page maturity.

sources.quote: most important thing with comment baiting is that it looks on brand and looks human. It has to relate to the niche that the creator is in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented engagement-baiting tactic for any platform (§5 GAP). This is a traffic/distribution gap. Palm's Telegram-routed manual posting (§5) could incorporate comment baiting as an Amin task.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a comment-baiting step to the Threads posting SOP: 1–10 baits/day for new accounts scaling to a max of 25–30 for mature accounts. Include a brief checklist (niche-relevant, human tone, linked to original post) in the Telegram task instructions sent to Amin.

new_findings.topic: AI model IG account inspiration sourcing
new_findings.title: 1-to-3 ratio filter: only clone IG accounts whose views outpace follower count

variants.claim: Copying an Instagram model whose recent reels have a views-to-followers ratio of at least 1:3 (views ≥ 3× followers) ensures you're piggybacking on a genuinely viral template rather than a stagnant one.
variants.what_they_do: Before cloning or face-swapping any source account, operator audits the last 7 reels and checks that total/average views exceed followers by a factor of 3. Accounts with views below follower count are skipped ('negative ratio'). This is the first filter before any content work begins.

sources.quote: what you're looking for is identify someone that's actually pumping on Instagram so you can benefit from it… ratio of one to three

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) scrapes inspo reels and scores them with AI vision (viral score), but the documented filter is engagement-signal-based, not an explicit views-to-followers ratio gate. The 1:3 ratio is a fast, human-readable pre-filter that could complement or precede Palm's AI scoring step. Palm's pipeline applies to real-creator social content; this tactic is described in an AI-creator context but the ratio logic is platform-agnostic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a views-to-followers ratio check (≥1:3 on last 7 reels) as a hard filter in the Apify scrape → Inspiration queue step (§1, step 1-2). Surface this metric in the inspo review UI alongside the existing viral score so editors and admins can triage faster. Applicable to both real and AI creator pipelines.

new_findings.title: Master one marketing channel completely before adding others

variants.claim: Agencies that spread effort across many marketing channels become mediocre at all of them; depth in one channel outperforms breadth across many.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's second top lesson: pick one traffic/marketing method and become the best at it before expanding. He acknowledges the risk of single-channel dependency (e.g., TikTok declining) but frames transferable skills as the safety net — skills learned on TikTok apply to Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts. The discipline is still to go deep on one channel first, then adapt skills to adjacent channels when the primary shows diminishing returns.

sources.quote: lesson two is always going to go back to marketing so many people are trying to do everything yeah just focus on one thing and become the best at it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is built around Instagram/Reels as the primary channel with some TikTok. The inspo-scraping system is IG-centric. This is aligned with Dylan's advice. However, Palm has no documented decision framework for when to add a new channel or deprecate an underperforming one. The gap is strategic channel governance, not channel selection itself.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's primary channel as Instagram Reels (current de facto state) and define explicit criteria for adding a second channel — e.g., 'when the IG pipeline is running at full cadence for 90 days with consistent growth, evaluate Reddit or TikTok as channel two.' This prevents premature channel sprawl and gives the team clear focus. Also document skill-transfer logic: the IG Reels inspo pipeline (§1) is reusable for TikTok and YouTube Shorts — make this explicit so editors understand the transferability.

new_findings.title: Charge $1,500–$2,500/month per client to hit $10K–$20K with 4–8 clients

variants.claim: The sweet spot for agency pricing is $1,500–$2,500/month, allowing a solo operator to hit $10K/month with just four clients rather than needing 50+ low-ticket clients.
variants.what_they_do: Targets $1,500–$2,500/month retainers per client. At $2,500 x 4 clients = $10K/month; 8 clients = $20K/month. One person can manage up to 25–26 clients at this tier before needing to hire.

sources.quote: 1,500 to 2,500, which is a nice sweet spot, you can. Because let's do the math here. 4 * 2,500 10K.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's commission structure is % of creator revenue (§7), not a flat retainer — so pricing model differs structurally. However, Palm has no documented rationale for its split percentages or floor minimums (§7 🔲 GAP). The 'fewer high-value clients beats many low-value clients' logic maps directly to Palm's creator portfolio sizing question.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use the $1,500–$2,500 benchmark as a reference when modeling minimum viable creator revenue per account Palm should onboard. Define a floor: if a creator is unlikely to generate enough revenue to justify Palm's management overhead, don't sign them. Document the commission split rationale explicitly to fill the §7 gap.

new_findings.title: Productize one repeatable service per niche so delivery is copy-paste, not custom each time

variants.claim: Offering a single systematized, repeatable service instead of custom work for every client is what makes an agency scalable and sustainable.
variants.what_they_do: Picks one niche and one core service (e.g., Google Ads lead gen to a landing page). Builds the campaign once, then copies and pastes the structure for every new client. Learns from aggregate campaign data across all clients to improve results over time. Explicitly avoids mixing service types (websites for one client, Google Ads for another, Facebook for a third).

sources.quote: once we've decided on the niche, we want to offer productized services, which means a service that we can offer again and again in a very systematized, very automated way

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social content pipeline (§1) is already productized — inspo scrape → analyze → film → edit → post runs the same way for every creator. The OF vault/PPV side (§2) is thin and non-productized. This finding reinforces Palm's existing strength on social and flags that PPV content production needs a similar systematized playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply the same copy-paste productization logic to OF vault/PPV content (§2 gap): define a standard PPV content set structure (e.g., X pieces per week, price tiers, release cadence) that can be templated across all creators rather than decided ad hoc per creator.

new_findings.topic: Target client qualification by company size
new_findings.title: Qualify prospects by employee count as a proxy for $1M–$10M revenue range

variants.claim: Businesses with 2–30 employees are typically in the $1M–$10M revenue range — large enough to afford agency services, small enough that you can reach the decision-maker directly.
variants.what_they_do: Uses employee headcount as a quick qualifier: ~2–3 employees signals ~$1M revenue; ~20–30 employees signals ~$10M. This range means the prospect has marketing budget AND the owner/decision-maker is still reachable without going through corporate gatekeepers.

sources.quote: at the 1 million mark, we'll say two to three employees. And then you can just do the math and work that up to 20 to 30 on the high end

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's creator acquisition is entirely Josh-DM-led with no documented qualification criteria (§6 🔲 GAP). There is no equivalent 'signal' Palm uses to decide which creators to pursue. The underlying logic — identify a proxy signal that predicts both willingness to pay AND accessibility — is directly applicable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a Palm creator qualification scorecard analogous to employee-count proxy: e.g., existing follower count range, current OF revenue floor, niche fit, posting frequency. Creators too small won't generate enough revenue; creators too large may already have management. Document this to systematize Josh's outbound decisions (§6).

new_findings.title: One person can manage ~25 clients before needing to hire; use contractors/white-label before full-time staff

variants.claim: A solo agency operator can sustainably manage 25–26 clients at premium pricing before the workload justifies building a contractor or outsourcer team.
variants.what_they_do: Ran 25–26 clients solo (described as 'probably excessive'). At that point, brought in freelancers, outsourcers, and contractors — not full-time employees. Points to white-label fulfillment as a scaling mechanism that avoids direct hiring overhead.

sources.quote: the most clients that I ever ran with just myself was like 25, 26 clients. It was probably excessive, but the money was there, so it justified it. And then at that point, I started looking for help

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently has Josh, Evan, Amin (contractor), editors, and an external chat team (§10). The team structure is thin and not formally documented. There is no scaling plan or org chart (§10 🔲 GAP). The contractor-before-FTE model Palm already implicitly uses (Amin, external chat) aligns with this advice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's explicit scaling model: at what creator/revenue count does each next hire (editor, VA, account manager, chatter) get triggered? Use the '25 clients solo' benchmark as a reference point. Formalize the contractor-first hiring ladder before Palm's creator roster grows to a point where ad hoc decisions create ops gaps.

new_findings.title: Six-step ad script for qualified creator leads

variants.claim: A repeatable six-step ad script (attention, key benefit, without-the-pain, proof, time-sensitive offer, downside of inaction) structures creative for conversion rather than ad-hoc messaging.
variants.what_they_do: Uses a fixed 6-step structure with one chosen angle (speed, risk-reduction, or ease); language must be coherent from ad to VSL to funnel to call and matched to a specific avatar (e.g. creators already doing 10k/mo) to avoid attracting low-quality opportunity-seekers.

sources.quote: the six-step ad script looks like this... number one, attention... number six is the downside of inaction

palm_comparison.vs_us: No acquisition messaging framework or avatar definition exists at Palm (§6 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 6-step script and define a concrete creator avatar (revenue band, follower count) so Palm outreach and any future content speaks one coherent, qualifying message.

consensus_risers.title: Score every chatter on 5 KPIs to build a leaderboard before restructuring

variants.claim: Pulling 30 days of chat logs and scoring each chatter on response time, PPV buy rate, average fan spend, retention, and objection handling creates an objective leaderboard that drives all restructuring decisions.
variants.what_they_do: Pull last 30 days of chat logs for every chatter. Score each individual across: response time, PPV buy rate, average fan spend, retention rate, objection handling quality. Aggregate into a single leaderboard score per chatter. Use that ranked list as the sole basis for warnings, fires, and promotions.

variants.claim: Chatters underperforming is typically caught too late because there is no proactive weekly tracking system — by the time revenue drops, the damage is already done.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan embeds chat performance as one of the seven required pieces of a delivery operating system: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator compliance, creator complaints. The chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality as their primary number. Weekly scorecard review catches a declining chatter before the month closes. He contrasts reactive delivery (everyone panics after revenue drops) with proactive delivery (problem spotted before it becomes painful via data and tracking).

variants.claim: A 1:10 ratio of subscription revenue to DM/tip revenue is the core chatting KPI, and chatters should not be hired until the agency earns $2–5K/month to ensure they're affordable.
variants.what_they_do: Chatting ratio defined as: for every $1 in subscription revenue, target $10 in DM+tip revenue. Benchmarks: 1:6 = decent, 1:8 = good, 1:10 = great, 1:15 = excellent. Hire VA chatters at $3–$5/hour + commission once agency hits $2–5K/month. Manage chatters via Discord. Welcome message framework: thank sub, ask their name, build rapport → send teasers → sell PPVs → upsell. Chatting is treated as a strategy, not ad-hoc.

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

sources.quote: pull the last 30 days of chat logs for every single chatter and score them individually
you now have a list of all your chatters with an overall score. You now have a leaderboard
you spot the chatter that's performance is declining before the month closes out and the revenue drops
A chatter misses standards and nothing happens. That's the culture.
the chatter start to underperform. Someone notices too late cuz the chat manager slacked off
for every $1 you have in the chats, you want to have $10 in the or in the subs. You want to have $10 in the chats. So we stick to 1 to 10
once you're paying or making 2 to 5K a month, hire a VA to chat. So, if you're making, you know, 1K a month, 700 bucks a month, do not hire chatters
add up your messages and tips as well and divide it by your subscriptions

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal chatter KPI framework or leaderboard. This audit methodology is exactly the kind of build-toward intelligence needed when Palm moves to an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this 5-KPI audit from day one. Define the scoring rubric before hiring chatter #1 so the leaderboard is operational at scale. Consider requiring the external chat team to report these same KPIs now as a baseline.

consensus_risers.title: Tie each team role's compensation to the specific business metric that role drives

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

variants.claim: Any metric that no one on the team explicitly owns will default back to the founder, making him the bottleneck and preventing scale.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan assigns a specific owned number to every role: setter owns qualified booked calls; closer owns cash collected; chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality; account manager owns creator retention and content compliance; creative director owns post volume and content performance; ops manager owns scorecard completion and weekly execution rhythm. He uses a 5-level accountability ladder: (1) wait to be told, (2) ask what to do, (3) recommend and act, (4) act then inform, (5) full ownership / exceptions only. Chatter managers should be at level 3-4; ops/sales managers at level 5. The coaching method to move someone from level 2→3: 'Before you come to me with a problem, bring me the problem, three options, and your recommendation.'

sources.quote: media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.
every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result
Every person on your team should own a number because any number nobody owns defaults back to you
Level three, recommend act. These people, they come to you and say, here's the problem. Here's the options. Here's my recommendation. Do you agree?
the setter owns qualified calls, the closer owns cash collected, the chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's team structure is thin (§10): Amin is a contractor, editors exist, and an external chat team is outsourced. No documented incentive structure or performance-linked comp bands exist. This is a GAP as Palm begins building in-house teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When hiring the first in-house chatters (§8 roadmap) and any additional editors or marketing staff, design role-specific KPIs with a variable comp component tied to those KPIs (e.g., chatters earn a bonus % of PPV revenue above a baseline; editors earn a bonus per post that hits a reach threshold). Document comp bands in the org structure build-out.

consensus_risers.title: Promote staff up the five levels of accountability to remove yourself as bottleneck

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.
delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation
if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no hiring scorecards, accountability framework, or management cadence documented (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the five-level accountability ladder as a management standard, push leads to level 4-5 with SOP-building expectations, and run Mon-Fri morning leadership calls.

consensus_risers.title: A defined niche, not looks or tech, separates earners

variants.claim: The biggest driver of five-figure outcomes is a hyper-specific model niche selling a defined fantasy and connection, not production quality or attractiveness.
variants.what_they_do: Reject 'basic blonde' generic models; build niche verticals (nurse, air-hostess, alt/gamer, ethnicity, trans) so 'fans feel she's for them', raising perceived value and scarcity; enforce that the niche/branding stays consistent across bio, highlights, reels, names and the Fanvue vault.

variants.claim: Most OFM agencies confuse 'brand' with 'niche,' but a real creator brand answers three questions: who is your audience, what is the creator's unique identity, and what story/fantasy are you selling.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant uses a three-question brand framework — (1) specific audience demographic beyond 'men who like porn', (2) unique creator identity that inspires devotion, (3) specific fantasy/story and emotional levers to pull — and claims this framework has taken creators from zero to top 0.1% earning hundreds of thousands per month. He cites Camila and Bonnie Blue as examples of brand-driven success.

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

sources.quote: It's the niche
you're selling connection and fantasy
most of OFM doesn't know what brand actually means or confuses the concept of brand with niche
brand and only fans boils down to three questions. The first question is who is your audience? The second question is what is your identity? The third and final question is what is your story?
make it that her persona

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1 MATURE) decides what to film from viral data but is not framed around per-creator niche/fantasy positioning; positioning is implicit, not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an explicit niche/positioning step to the inspo pipeline for real creators and enforce cross-platform branding consistency; high-value overlap with current work.

consensus_risers.title: Revenue-gated hiring ladder: VAs, then chat manager, then ops

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

variants.claim: A practical delegation trigger for a horizontal agency is one account manager hired for every 10–20 creators, focused purely on client communication and routine tasks.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter's explicit hiring cadence: start solo, outsource chatting/content to a chatting agency immediately, hire first internal person when workload is unmanageable (focused on creator comms and daily duties), then scale at 1 hire per 10–20 creators. At 16 creators with in-house chatting, he calculates ~48 chatters (3 per creator) plus 4+ managers for oversight pods of 10.

sources.quote: you need to hire managers
10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.
Once I surpass the 10 model mark, I'm going to start hiring one person for every 10 models to keep up with the client communication and all the duties that they need to take on.
You need about 48 people that you need to manage. Of those 48 people, you're going to need pods of 10 with management.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin with no documented hiring criteria, comp bands, or scaling plan (§10). This is a ready-made staffing/comp ladder.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this ladder as a template for Palm's org-scaling plan; map Palm roles (Amin, editors, chat) onto the stages and define revenue gates for next hires.

consensus_risers.title: Personalized openers using username or profile detail to force engagement

variants.claim: Sending a message personalized to a specific subscriber's username or visible profile detail dramatically increases response rates versus generic openers.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to craft a unique opener for each sub that contains a play on the subscriber's username (e.g., 'Hey Jimmy, are you the same Jimmy Johnson from the Dallas Cowboys?'). The opener must include exactly one question — no more — so it opens a single conversational thread the sub feels obligated to answer. Copy-paste openers like 'hey how are you' or 'thanks for the follow' are explicitly banned. The opener is designed to (a) prove a human is typing, (b) put the chatter in conversational control by triggering a reaction, and (c) give the sub an easy, interesting thing to respond to.

variants.claim: The PPV caption is the last decision point before purchase; embedding specific, timestamped curiosity hooks (rather than generic hype) dramatically increases unlock rates by making the sub feel something personalized and unmissable is inside.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters/copywriters craft captions ending with one or more of these proven curiosity hooks: (1) Timestamped surprise — 'I can't believe I did that at 2:41' (specificity signals authenticity); (2) Personalized connection — 'I want to see if you can spot which part I was thinking about you'; (3) Challenge/gamification — 'There's no way you'll last past what I did at minute 1:21'; (4) Embarrassment/vulnerability — 'I'm pretty embarrassed I did that towards the end, but oh well'; (5) Physical detail — 'I'm still sore from that last part'; (6) Multi-content choice — 'Which one of those positions was your favorite?' These are used as the final sentence only; the rest of the caption continues the sexting/emotional build-up from the conversation. Specificity (exact timestamps, specific details) is emphasised as what makes hooks feel genuine vs scripted.

sources.quote: send a personalized opener that is unique to the person who is receiving it
it must contain one question but no more than one question
I wonder if you'll see the surprise I threw in there for you
I'm still sore from that last part
when you're specific, it seems genuine

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chat team (§8); there is no internal SOP or opener framework at Palm. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm builds its in-house chat team, mandate personalized username-based openers as the baseline standard. Create a training doc that (1) defines what counts as a valid personalized opener, (2) lists banned copy-paste patterns, and (3) drills the one-question rule. Include this in pre-training screening — candidates who default to 'hey how are you' should be filtered.

consensus_risers.title: Four-axis sub qualification (profession, location, age, hobbies) to size wallet and build rapport

variants.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
variants.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. They ask about: (1) Profession — men spend 40+ hrs/week on work and will talk about it freely; also signals income level. (2) Location — dictates tone, cultural references, and timezone. (3) Age — a 52-year-old has 30 more years of earning history than a 22-year-old; older subs get more formal language, younger subs get slang. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.

variants.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
variants.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.

sources.quote: number one profession you will be talking to men okay simps Subs they are men
another one is age how old are they part of the reason of qualifying is that we want to see their potential as a customer
what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm for chatter qualification frameworks (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a qualification checklist into the in-house chatter training SOP: chatters must capture profession, location, age, and at least one hobby for every sub before escalating to selling. Store this data per-fan in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9) to enable personalized re-engagement. Age and profession data should also feed the whale-hunting logic — a 50-year-old professional is a higher-priority cultivation target than an 18-year-old student.

consensus_risers.title: Weekly async Loom chat reviews focused on new subscribers + high-ticket purchases

variants.claim: Regular async video reviews (via Loom) of specific conversation types — new subscribers, purchases, high-ticket sales — plus chatter-submitted chats drive measurable skill improvement.
variants.what_they_do: Manager opens the 'new subscribers' tab and reviews those conversations each week. Also reviews purchase logs and high-ticket sales. Asks each chatter to submit specific chats for review. Records Loom feedback videos. Per-review focus stays on one improvement point. Maintains 'scientist mode' (critical, constructive) not 'dictator mode' (anger-driven). Each chatter gets one weekly improvement point plus actionable steps.

variants.claim: A live audit of a creator's chats reliably surfaces significant revenue being left on the table, making chat optimization a strong acquisition wedge and a high-value service.
variants.what_they_do: Offers a free 'chat teardown' on the sales call to show exactly where money is being lost in the DMs (reply cadence, scripts, welcome messages, account setup, day-ladder), then offers to install the system; treats chatting as the core monetization service.

sources.quote: We open up the new subscribers tab and read those conversations. We look at purchases. We look for high ticket sales. And we ask chatters every week to give us specific chats to review
Every week we analyze the one point of improvement for each chatter
you're showing them where the actual money is being left in the chats
how often you should reply... some example messages... a script... a simple day ladder

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal review cadence at Palm (§8, outsourced). Partially relevant to the AI chatting project as well. Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a weekly review ritual into the in-house chat SOP from day one: manager records a ≤5-min Loom per chatter, logs the single focus point and action steps in Airtable (add a 'Chatter Reviews' table under the chat team's workspace).

consensus_risers.title: Brief chatters on model persona: tone, emojis, typing quirks, niche

variants.claim: Chatters must mirror the model's real personality and typing style (excessive letters, cute emojis, pet names) so subscribers cannot detect a VA is behind the keyboard.
variants.what_they_do: The persona section of the training PDF specifies: age range of the model → inferred typing style (e.g. 18–24 = lots of cute emojis, elongated words like 'heyyyy'), the model's content niche, any pet names she uses, and other personality markers. Chatter applies all of these verbatim.

variants.claim: The single highest-impact revenue intervention at $50K+/mo is an audit of chatting: verify mass messages and scripts match the creator's personality sheet, and fire chatters whose pages are underutilized — this caused a same-day revenue spike.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin and Jason logged into Barry's Inflow (chatting platform) as admins and ran a three-part audit: (1) identified what was wrong with chatting mechanics; (2) verified all mass messages and automation scripts correlated with each creator's personality sheet; (3) identified and fired unnecessary chatters — reducing from 6 to 4 — freeing the remaining chatters to work larger, more active pages. Revenue spiked the same day.

sources.quote: take note of the client's Persona so if the girl is aged between say 18 to 24 then using a lot of cute emojis when typing using a lot of excessive letters in some words
we went through all your mass messages we first identified what was wrong with your chatting and we looked at the correlation between all your personality sheets of your clients with the chatting
firing unnecessary chatters because you had like six chatters at the time when you really only needed four

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and there is no documented persona-briefing process internally. When Palm builds in-house or AI chatting, voice consistency will be critical — especially for AI personas (§3) where breaking character destroys the illusion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'chatter voice card' field to the Creators table in Airtable (and AI Account Profile for AI personas). Populate: age, typing quirks, signature emojis, pet names, niche. Feed this card to any chatter (human or AI) at session start. Especially high priority for AI persona chatting.

consensus_risers.title: Build a model profile doc with chatting style, persona facts, and content inventory for chatters

variants.claim: A documented model profile — covering chatting tone, physical stats, hobbies, and personality details — is essential so chatters can maintain consistent fan relationships without breaking immersion.
variants.what_they_do: For every creator they build a profile that includes: existing chatting style (especially for account transitions), height, age, bra size, feet size, astrological sign, hobbies, and other facts that come up naturally in DM conversations. Chatters reference this doc before and during conversations. Content is stored in a shared Dropbox/Google Drive the model can also upload to directly.

variants.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
variants.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.

sources.quote: you need to understand their chatting style if especially if they're running their existing of account and you need to transition their chatting style to your Chatters
little things like their height age bra size feet size astrological sign hobbies things that just naturally get brought up during these conversations
within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's external chat team operates without a documented Palm-side model profile SOP (§8 — outsourced, no internal SOP). Fan Tracker CRM (§9) collects fan data but there is no equivalent creator-side profile that briefs chatters on the model's persona and voice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'Model Brief' Airtable record linked to each creator in the Creators table. Fields: chatting tone/style notes, physical stats, hobbies, boundaries, content categories available. Share with external chat team now; becomes the foundation for in-house chatter onboarding when that roadmap item ships (§8 build-toward).

consensus_risers.title: Per-source tracking links expose where revenue originates

variants.claim: Operators run the funnel as a tracked numbers game: per-platform tracking links reveal where subs and revenue actually come from and surface conversion bottlenecks.
variants.what_they_do: A separate Fanvue tracking link per social account measures clicks, subs, revenue and per-source CTR; a low CTR (under 1-2%) flags a broken bio/CTA, X funnel or Fanvue setup to fix; spend-per-sub is calculated per source to decide where to invest time and money.

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: a direct tracking link created on fan view for X only
A good CTR is 3 to 5%. Less than 1 to 2% means your funnel's weak
the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm tracks invoicing/data (§7, §9) and has Fan Tracker, but cross-channel sub attribution and funnel-leak diagnostics are not documented; traffic measurement is a gap (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement per-source tracking links plus a per-channel CTR / spend-per-sub view for real creators to attribute OF subs to their social source and diagnose funnel leaks.

consensus_risers.title: Weekly cadence and standards prevent regression to chaos

variants.claim: A fixed weekly operating cadence plus enforced standards (recognition and consequences) keeps an agency from regressing into reactive firefighting.
variants.what_they_do: Installs a Monday hour-one reset (look back, look forward, pick 1-3 priorities, schedule, overflow), a weekly single 'game tape' root-cause review, decision-only compressed 30-min meetings, and a recognition/consequence system; 'culture is what you tolerate and what you reinforce.'

variants.claim: Weekly payouts ('weekly pouch') keep both the agency and creator motivated and signal operational professionalism early in the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After onboarding, agency moves to a weekly payout cadence. Framed as both a financial rhythm and a morale tool — seeing money arrive weekly reinforces creator buy-in and agency credibility. No detailed reporting format described, but the weekly cadence is presented as standard practice.

sources.quote: Without rhythm, everything will regress into chaos
Culture is what you tolerate and culture is what you reinforce
after a week or two, we do weekly pouch, just to let you know — that's just a cool little tip, enjoy the money coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm coordinates via Telegram with no documented operating cadence, scorecards, or standards-enforcement rhythm (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Install a lightweight weekly cadence (priorities reset + one root-cause review) and a recognition/consequence standard across Palm's team and contractors.

consensus_risers.title: Scrape a 100k-subreddit master list keyed to creator physical attributes, then niche-match posts

variants.claim: Rather than manually searching for relevant subreddits, build a scraped database of 100,000+ subreddits tagged by niche attributes so any new creator can be immediately mapped to every viable subreddit based on her physical characteristics.
variants.what_they_do: Agency built (or uses) a scraper that pulls every subreddit and tags it by niche (ethnicity, body type, fetish, etc.). When a creator is onboarded, her attributes (blonde, big breasts, e-girl, etc.) are matched against the database to generate her posting list. Volume over precision: one piece of content gets posted across every marginally relevant subreddit. Key insight: NSFW content also gets cross-posted into SFW subreddits (makeup, car repair, GeoGuessr) using curiosity/cleavage tactics to reach users who have never enabled NSFW settings.

variants.claim: Content quality is the single most important Reddit variable — good content can overcome mediocre subreddits, but bad content fails even in perfect subreddits.
variants.what_they_do: Start with a niche (e.g. feet), find subreddits by searching and crawling competitor model pages. Post in each; if a subreddit doesn't reach ~20+ upvotes, swap it for a new one. Simultaneously test content by finding top-performing posts within target subreddits and asking the creator to recreate them with a personal twist. Scale to 20 subreddits/account then plateau for a month before expanding further.

sources.quote: what we would do is we would just have a scraper that would basically scrape every single subreddit that would or could be tied to either one of these niches. You'd build up a list of subreddits. You'd have a 100,000 subreddits

there's also a lot of say for work subreddits where people would just, you know, I don't know, ask for advice for piercings in their ears or or makeup advice. And you could also post in these subreddits
we basically test the subreddit… if it doesn't get like 20 plus uploads a day then we just change them out for new ones

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit presence (§5 GAP). Palm's content pipeline (§1) does use Apify scraping for IG inspiration, so the scraping capability exists — it would need to be redirected to Reddit subreddit discovery. The SFW infiltration angle is completely absent from any Palm workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Commission a one-time scrape of Reddit subreddits tagged by adult-content niche to build a master lookup table. Store in Airtable alongside creator attribute fields. On creator onboarding, auto-generate a subreddit posting list. Prioritise NSFW subreddits first; layer in SFW curiosity subreddits (lifestyle, hobby) only once the core strategy is proven.

consensus_risers.topic: Platform-native content formats for AI model social
consensus_risers.title: Format-match by platform: long-caption storytelling on IG, slideshows on TikTok, text posts on Threads

variants.claim: Each platform rewards a specific content format; matching format to platform algorithm dramatically increases reach and watch time for AI model accounts.
variants.what_they_do: Instagram: long-caption 'storytelling' posts where the persona narrates a relatable story (ex-drama etc.) — increases watch time which triggers broader algorithmic push; captions generated via ChatGPT from real OFI model examples. Also 'caption hook videos' with an on-screen text hook. TikTok: slideshow format prioritized because TikTok is actively promoting slides and they go viral more easily; targets lonely-men content angles. Threads: short punchy text posts ('somebody FaceTime me') that feel real and prompt engagement from the target demographic.

variants.claim: Carousel posts move the needle most on Threads, but text/comment posts are needed as cheap filler to hit daily volume targets.
variants.what_they_do: TDM designates carousel posts as their primary growth format ('goats') and text/comment posts as filler. Of the 5–8 daily posts, at least a couple are carousels; the rest are short text posts. They prioritise content that is shareable and/or controversial as the single biggest lever.

sources.quote: Find some good storytelling post from OFI models on IG and use chat GTP to make your own
when it comes to Tik Tok you're going to want to do more slides because Tik Tok really favors slides
comment posts as your fillers and then carousel posts as the goats. These are the kind of things that are really going to move the needle with your page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong at inspo-scraping and replicating viral Reels but is built around video for IG. The Airtable pipeline does not appear to have a Threads text-post workflow or TikTok slideshow format lane. Content format strategy is not documented per-platform.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's content pipeline (§1) to include platform-specific format lanes: (1) TikTok slideshow template workflow, (2) Threads text-post templates. For real creators, these formats can supplement the existing Reels-first approach. For AI personas, add ChatGPT-assisted long-caption storytelling as a content type in the `Inspiration`/`Assets` pipeline.

consensus_risers.title: Vertical vs. horizontal scale: choose model before building ops

variants.claim: OFM agencies must consciously choose between vertical scale (few creators, salary model, high margin, high risk) and horizontal scale (many creators, rev-share, lower margin, lower risk) because the ops, team size, and marketing obligations are fundamentally different.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter maps two distinct agency architectures: vertical = 1–few salary creators, agency keeps ~90% of revenue, agency owns all marketing; horizontal = many rev-share creators who self-market with agency-provided SOPs, agency takes ~30% cut plus upsell fees. He recommends starting horizontal, then using that cash flow to fund a vertical page for higher margins.

variants.claim: Acquiring two high-paying clients requires far less effort than acquiring fifty low-paying ones, making low-ticket volume models a trap for agency owners.
variants.what_they_do: Frames the math explicitly: 50 clients at $97/month = $5,000; 4 clients at $2,500 = $10,000 with a fraction of the acquisition and support overhead. Argues that there is 'not really that much extra work' to target higher-paying clients but the upside is dramatically better.

sources.quote: There are two ways that you can scale an OFM company. Okay? You can either vertically scale.
I would take the income that I'm making from that operation and then start injecting it into vertical scale models on a vertical scale page and have the best of both worlds.
getting two clients, even at a higher price point, is orders of magnitude easier than getting 20, 30, 40, 50 lower paying clients

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently operates as a hybrid leaning vertical — real creators with a revenue-split model, Palm owning social/content and outsourcing chat. Palm's commission structure is per-creator at onboarding (§7) but no documented strategic rationale for vertical vs. horizontal positioning. No explicit decision framework exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's explicit strategic choice between vertical and horizontal, or define a hybrid model. If Palm wants to scale to many creators, formalize the horizontal SOP-provision model (optimize what creators already have). If Palm stays vertical with fewer high-value creators, document the salary-vs-split decision criteria. At minimum, use this framework in founder strategy sessions.

consensus_risers.title: Multi-stage hiring funnel with hard filters before any offer

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

sources.quote: set up a direct hiring funnel that may even take like a week to get all the applicants
we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring is not formally documented (§10 — THIN). There is no written hiring funnel, scorecard, or onboarding SOP. Josh currently drives acquisition and ops largely himself, which matches the 'doing it yourself while you find the right person' fallback, but the structured multi-stage filter does not exist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a formal 2–3 stage hiring funnel for each role Palm needs next (e.g., editor, chatter, VA): job post → async task/test → live screen → offer. Define a written 'must-have' scorecard per role before opening any search. Document the cost-of-bad-hire math internally so the team doesn't rush to fill seats.

variants.claim: Every chat should build rapport and let the customer initiate the sexual turn before selling, never opening with the sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard trains chatters on a fixed framework (general conversation/rapport, transition to a sexual conversation, then dirty talk leading into PPV) and lets the customer initiate so the model holds the power position; habibi similarly builds rapport and conserves energy rather than blasting horny messages or mass-message bundles.

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

new_evidence.title: Tag every fan by relationship type and price accordingly

variants.claim: High-converting chat operations segment every subscriber into named relationship/spender tiers and differentiate content, pricing and chatter effort by tier.
variants.what_they_do: Four fan types (transactional, lover, whale, time-waster) marked with emoji labels in Fanvue fan-notes and placed into platform lists; mass-PPV sent per list excluding whales; same content sold at different prices by tier; whales sold to 1:1 at high anchors, never via mass message.

variants.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).

sources.quote: Mark all spenders on relationship types
Target each group with specific mass messages
if we were talking to, say, a low or non-spender, we want to be able to transition in a way that is fast and efficient and make that first pay-per-view seem like a no-brainer
it's pretty much exclusively for non-active high spenders. So people that used to spend a lot of money, but don't really talk anymore

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data collection (§9 PARTIAL) but chatting is outsourced (§8) with no in-house fan-tiering SOP or differential-pricing model; pricing is also a gap (§7).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a fan-tier taxonomy plus differential-PPV pricing ladder into the in-house/AI chatting roadmap, fed from existing Fan Tracker data now; pricing applies to real creators today.

new_evidence.title: Log personal fan details in notes so any chatter can continue the relationship seamlessly

variants.claim: Chatters add personal details (job, location, interests) to each fan's notes or rename field so any chatter who picks up the conversation later can maintain the illusion of a continuous, invested relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After a fan shares personal information in DMs, the chatter immediately records it in that fan's notes inside the OF messaging interface (or an external CRM). The fan's name field may be renamed to include key tags. When a different chatter takes over a shift, they review notes first and reference those personal details naturally — the fan feels remembered and valued, which increases LTV.

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

sources.quote: we'll have our chatting team add notes to each fan where you can write down personable information and the next chatter that hops on to talk to that dude will kind of have an idea
maybe he's told the girl what he does for a living where he lives and we'll put all that information in his notes or in his name
building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9), but no written retention/chatter handoff SOP. The notes-per-fan mechanic described here is a direct operational input for the Fan Tracker and for the in-house chat team SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalise a 'fan card' field set in the Fan Tracker (or OF notes) — at minimum: spending tier, personal details shared, last PPV purchased, last contact date. Require the external chat team to populate this today; make it mandatory in the in-house chat SOP. Aligns with and extends §9 whale-hunting data.

new_evidence.title: Hold weekly video calls with each creator to give account updates and collect feedback

new_evidence.topic: Fan connection before subscription as LTV driver
new_evidence.title: Build parasocial fan connection via social persona BEFORE the OF subscribe to raise LTV

variants.claim: Fans who feel connected to a creator's persona — trusting her, finding her funny, being attracted to her personality — before they subscribe spend more and stay longer, measurably raising LTV 20–30% vs. content-first pages.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's agency builds a distinct social persona for each creator so fans are 'invested before they even spend money.' The persona is expressed across IG/TikTok (top-of-funnel), and the brand promise is consistent with what fans find on OF. This is tracked: they observe LTV and CVR lift of 20–30% after implementing persona-first strategy.

variants.claim: Conversion on the paid platform is treated like buying any product: visible likes/reviews validate purchase, so operators manufacture social proof before driving traffic.
variants.what_they_do: Compares a profile with no likes to a one-star restaurant; boosts likes (e.g. creating throwaway Fanvue accounts via temp-mail to like wall posts) so the page looks established and trusted, alongside an optimized profile pic and multi-angle banner that show the model fully before subscribing.

sources.quote: if you can create that connection before the only fans like they're invested already before they even spend money that's what creates retention
I've seen my LTV go up like 20 30% along with CVR along with all these other metrics
Perform a like boost to give more social proofing
no likes equals poor conversion

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is strong on viral content distribution but the baseline doc does not document a persona-consistency check between social identity and OF page experience. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.
